critic_raw:
The design applies unsupervised anomaly detection (an autoencoder) to sensor data where fault labels are known and available. This creates a fundamental mismatch: the autoencoder is trained to reconstruct normal operating conditions and then thresholded to detect anomalies, but the evaluation uses labeled fault data. If labeled fault data is available, there is no principled reason to use unsupervised detection rather than a supervised classifier — the unsupervised framing is appropriate only when labels are scarce or unavailable, which is contradicted by the design's use of 'maintenance logs offering reliable ground truth.' The design does not justify why unsupervised methods are preferred when labeled training data exists.

The stratified random split on time-series sensor data with 12 months of readings has the same temporal leakage problem present throughout this case set: fault trajectories in industrial machinery develop over hours or days before the fault event, and a random split will place pre-fault sensor readings from the same degradation sequence in both training and evaluation sets. The autoencoder trains to reconstruct 'normal' patterns, but if some training observations are drawn from the same pre-fault sequences as test observations, the reconstruction error threshold calibration is contaminated.

The primary evaluation metric is Mean Time to Failure (MTTF), which measures the average time between failures — a system reliability metric typically computed over an entire operational period, not over a holdout test set. MTTF is appropriate for comparing the aggregate reliability of two systems over time, but for evaluating a single model's performance on a historical test set, it is not directly computable without a deployment scenario where the model's alerts actually affect maintenance decisions. The design conflates a deployment performance metric (MTTF over operational period) with an offline evaluation metric (model performance on historical test data).

The threshold selection for the autoencoder reconstruction error is a critical hyperparameter that directly determines the precision/recall tradeoff, yet the design does not specify how the threshold is set. If it is tuned on the validation set using labeled fault data, the model is effectively supervised during threshold selection, which undermines the unsupervised framing. If it is set by a fixed rule (e.g., 2-sigma from training reconstruction error distribution), the threshold may be poorly calibrated for the specific fault types in the dataset.

The baseline threshold system is described as undergoing 'equivalent tuning' — but threshold-based systems for industrial sensors are typically tuned by domain engineers with knowledge of equipment physics, not by data-driven optimization. If the threshold baseline is tuned by data-driven methods on the same validation set as the autoencoder, the comparison advantage for the autoencoder is obscured because both systems are data-driven at the threshold selection stage.
all_issues_raised: Applying unsupervised anomaly detection when labeled fault data is explicitly available and used for evaluation is unjustified; the design does not explain why supervised fault classification is not used instead of an autoencoder, making the architectural choice unmotivated.

Stratified random split on 12 months of sensor data with temporal fault trajectories places pre-fault degradation readings from the same sequence in both training and test, contaminating the autoencoder's normal-behavior reconstruction baseline with near-fault patterns.

MTTF is a deployment reliability metric computed over an operational period, not an offline model evaluation metric; it cannot be computed from a historical holdout set without counterfactual knowledge of whether the model's alerts would have prevented the recorded failures.

The autoencoder's reconstruction error threshold is a critical decision boundary whose selection method is unspecified; threshold selection on labeled validation data makes the system effectively supervised at evaluation time, contradicting the unsupervised framing.

The threshold-based baseline 'equivalent tuning' is ambiguous: domain-calibrated thresholds derived from equipment physics are fundamentally different from data-driven thresholds, and treating them equivalently obscures whether the autoencoder advantage reflects learned patterns or merely better threshold calibration from data.
all_issues_adjudicated: Applying unsupervised anomaly detection when labeled fault data is explicitly available and used for evaluation is unjustified; the design does not explain why supervised fault classification is not used instead of an autoencoder, making the architectural choice unmotivated.

Stratified random split on 12 months of sensor data with temporal fault trajectories places pre-fault degradation readings from the same sequence in both training and test, contaminating the autoencoder's normal-behavior reconstruction baseline with near-fault patterns.

MTTF is a deployment reliability metric computed over an operational period, not an offline model evaluation metric; it cannot be computed from a historical holdout set without counterfactual knowledge of whether the model's alerts would have prevented the recorded failures.

The autoencoder's reconstruction error threshold is a critical decision boundary whose selection method is unspecified; threshold selection on labeled validation data makes the system effectively supervised at evaluation time, contradicting the unsupervised framing.

The threshold-based baseline 'equivalent tuning' is ambiguous: domain-calibrated thresholds derived from equipment physics are fundamentally different from data-driven thresholds, and treating them equivalently obscures whether the autoencoder advantage reflects learned patterns or merely better threshold calibration from data.
